assets.name: lecture_05_figure_01.png
assets.rationale: All four candidates are opening campus or title-card frames rather than lecture content. None shows blackboard mathematics, a diagram, or a classroom layout that would help reconstruct the notes, so this subtitle window should be skipped.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_02.png
assets.subtitle_excerpt: And l times m have exactly the same action on any vector, and therefore m times l is equal to l times m as operators.
assets.caption_hint: And l times m have exactly the same action on any vector, and therefore m times l is equal to l times m as operators.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: All four candidates show essentially the same clean board close-up, but this final frame is the safest choice because it preserves the fully written difference-quotient equation in a settled board state with no lecturer occlusion. It is sharp, complete, and directly useful for reconstructing the step leading to the time derivative.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: We get the time derivative of the state vector of the system.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This final frame is the cleanest settled view of the board. The lecturer has moved out of the way, the full left-hand derivative and central equality are unobstructed, and the expectation-value commutator expression is readable across the board. Earlier candidates are partially blocked by Susskind while he is still writing or moving.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: If you know what the Hamiltonian is as an operator or as a matrix,

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the best compromise between completeness and occlusion. The identifying line at the top connecting \(|i\rangle\) with the energy-eigenvector notation is still visible, and the lower expansion of the state as a sum over basis vectors is more legible here than in the earlier frames, where Susskind's arm blocks more of the equation. The last frame adds more body and eraser occlusion without revealing additional content.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: Let's go back to the original equation that the state vector is a sum over the eigenvectors.

assets.name: lecture_05_figure_06.png
assets.rationale: This is the strongest frame because it captures the finished board state for the moment: the field-direction sketch labeled \(B_z\), the Hamiltonian \(H=\omega\sigma_z/2\), and the full vertical list \(\sigma_x,\sigma_y,\sigma_z\). The lecturer has mostly moved out of the way, so the layout is clearer and more useful for reconstructing both the equation and the simple geometric setup.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: the z component in the Hamiltonian, but I want to find out how all the components, sigma x, sigma y, and sigma z, how they vary with time.
assets.caption_hint: magnetic field along z and spin components
